commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: The remaining action is maintainer judgment on Telegram Guest Mode defaults and authorization policy, not a narrow automated repair.; Needs attention: The diff does not introduce supply-chain or secret-handling risk, but it does add a new Telegram inbound authorization boundary that needs maintainer acceptance before merge. (sha=79f2bdb4e1869775fe90eea2c88bdaa931d1aabf)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds Telegram `guest_message` handling, guest-session routing, `answerGuestQuery` delivery, guest config/schema/docs, and focused Telegram tests.

Reproducibility: not applicable. as a bug reproduction: this PR adds a new Telegram feature. Current main lacks guest-message handling, while the PR body and screenshots supply after-fix live Telegram proof for the new path.
commands.review_followup: The remaining action is maintainer judgment on Telegram Guest Mode defaults and authorization policy, not a narrow automated repair.

target.title: feat(telegram): add guest mode
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw did not handle Telegram Guest Bot `guest_message` updates, so bots could not answer `@bot` guest queries from groups where the bot is not a member. - Why it matters: Guest Bots are a Telegram-supported way to let a bot answer from outside a group without joining it, but they require a different update type and response API than normal group messages. - What changed: Telegram now accepts `guest_message` updates, routes them through an isolated direct-style agent turn, preserves sender metadata, and delivers exactly one visible response through `answerGuestQuery`. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal Telegram group/direct message handling remains on the existing send path. Guest Mode does not inherit per-group policies or normal group conversation bindings; it uses account-level Telegram allowlist rules. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #79077 - Partially addresses #79077 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram bots can answer Guest Bot queries from group chats where the bot is not a member when `channels.telegram.guest.enabled` is enabled or auto-detected from Telegram bot metadata. - Real environment tested: Ubuntu Linux, local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway, Telegram channel configured, OpenAI Codex with GPT-5.5. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Activate the guest mode in the BotFather telegram bot settings. 2. Built OpenClaw from this branch. 3. Built Control UI assets. 4. Restarted the local gateway. 5. Sent a Telegram guest query to the bot from an external group where the bot was not a member. (`@openclawbot what time is it, can you read previous messages here? What is my telegram id, what is this channel / group id?`) 6. Verified that one visible answer appeared in Telegram. 7. Verified that users not allowed by the account-level allowlist could not use Guest Mode. 8. Tested groups where the bot is already a member to check that normal Telegram group handling did not regress. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): - Observed result after fix: The guest query created an isolated direct-style Telegram guest session and produced one visible Telegram answer. Unauthorized guest senders were blocked by account-level allowFrom. Normal groups where the bot is already a member continued to work through the existing Telegram message path. - What was not tested: Bot-to-bot mode, long-running multi-account (more than 3) production soak testing, and cross-platform release-package install proof. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, OpenClaw did not include guest_message in Telegram allowed updates and had no answerGuestQuery delivery path, so Guest Bot queries were not converted into agent turns. (So this did just not exist previously) ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: N/A for a new feature. Telegram Guest Bots use a new update and response shape that the existing Telegram channel did not support. - Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Telegram tests covered normal message updates, but not guest_message allowed updates, guest session isolation, or answerGuestQuery delivery. - Contributing context (if known): Guest Mode is similar to inline behavior from a product perspective, but Telegram delivers it as a guest message that must be answered through the Bot API guest-answer endpoint rather than the normal send-message path. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/webhook.test.ts - Scenario the test should lock in: A guest update is accepted, routed to an isolated guest session, keeps sender metadata available to the agent, does not inherit normal group bindings or group policies, and sends only one visible Telegram guest answer. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the Telegram plugin boundary where allowed updates, bot capability detection, guest routing, session context, authorization, and delivery behavior meet, without requiring live Telegram credentials. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A. New tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled with channels.telegram.guest.enabled. - true: handle Telegram guest queries. - false: ignore Telegram guest queries. - auto: handle guest queries when Telegram bot metadata reports guest-query support. Guest Mode uses the Telegram account-level allowlist. It intentionally does not apply per-group allowlists or normal group conversation bindings because the bot is not a member of the source group in the guest interaction. ## Diagram (if applicable) For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`. ```text Before: [Telegram guest_message] -> [not in allowed updates / no guest delivery path] -> [no visible answer] After: [Telegram guest_message] -> [account-level allowlist] -> [isolated direct-style guest session] -> [agent turn] -> [answerGuestQuery] -> [one visible Telegram answer] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) Yes. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No. - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) Yes. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No. - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No. - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: This adds a Telegram guest-response capability using Telegram Bot API answerGuestQuery. Guest access is gated by the existing Telegram account-level allowFrom list. Guest turns use isolated direct-style session keys (because this is basically a direct message (the bot does not have access to the group)), skip normal group bindings, and deliver only one visible response. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Ubuntu Linux. - Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway. - Model/provider: OpenAI Codex, GPT-5.5. - Integration/channel (if any): Telegram. - Relevant config (redacted): Telegram channel configured with Guest Mode enabled; bot token and account details redacted. ### Steps 1. Enable guest mode for the bot using the telegram BotFather. 2. Build OpenClaw from this branch. 3. Build Control UI assets. 4. Restart the local gateway. 5. Send a Telegram guest query to the bot from an external group where the bot is not a member. 6. Confirm that Telegram shows one visible bot answer. 7. Send a guest query from a user that is not allowed by account-level allowFrom. 8. Send normal messages in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Expected - Allowed guest queries create an isolated guest session and produce one visible Telegram answer. - Unauthorized guest senders are blocked. - Normal Telegram group handling still works in groups where the bot is already a member. ### Actual - Allowed guest queries produced one visible answer. - Unauthorized guest senders were blocked. - Existing normal Telegram group handling still worked. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [X] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Live Telegram Guest Mode round trip from a group where the bot was not a member. - Unauthorized guest sender blocked by account-level allowlist. - Normal Telegram groups where the bot is already a member still handled messages normally. - Control UI assets load after pnpm ui:build. - Edge cases checked: - Unauthorized guest sender fallback. - Guest isolation from normal group bindings. (Needs to remove bot in between from the group) - What you did not verify: - Bot-to-bot mode. - Long-running multi-account (>3) production soak. - Cross-platform release-package install proof. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. Local Codex review was run during the beta-branch iteration and again after moving the patch onto current `origin/main`. Actionable findings from the earlier rounds were addressed before opening the PR. The final main-based Codex review reported no discrete actionable correctness issues. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes. - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) Yes. - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Guest Mode could accidentally inherit normal group routing state. - Mitigation: Guest turns use isolated direct-style guest session keys and skip normal conversation bindings. - Risk: Telegram Guest Mode supports only one visible answer for this interaction shape. - Mitigation: Guest delivery sends only the first final answer through answerGuestQuery. - Risk: Unauthorized users could trigger guest turns. - Mitigation: Guest authorization uses the Telegram account-level allowFrom list. ~Horo
target.author: horotw
